speaker: interviewer
text: Michael Librach. We are here in Scarsdale, New York. Today is November 2, 1995, and I am Terry Toll. Michael, could you describe how the German invasion of Lodz struck you? Did you know that it was going to happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: We all expected that war would happen. A week before the invasion, as everybody knows, Molotov shook hands with Ribbentrop and at that point, war became inevitable. That moment found me at the seashore. I was vacationing and-- together with a group of friends. And we all jumped into the trains and went back home. And then September 1st came, which was Friday afternoon that I found out that we were being bombed and that wars have started. The first two, three days were normal. We didn't know too much what's going on. And then on the fifth day, on the 5th of September, in the morning, I was at that time, for some reason or other, sleeping in my sister's apartment. My sister was in, in New York. And her maid woke me up around very early in the morning and said all men are asked to leave the city. And I dressed and I went downstairs. And I went out and there was just a crowd of people walking west-- walking east, I'm sorry-- walking east out of the city. And I just joined the crowd and I was just walking. I had no idea. where, where we going. And it came out that we were going for about three days like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this an army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Towards Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this an army call up?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was just-- I think it was a rumor. The Germans were entering Lodz at that day on the fifth, and we were being pushed out. And not only men, but everybody walked out, an awful lot of people, women, children. And like in all war crisis, money meant absolutely nothing. And I remember that some people were-- had gold on them and to just get on a wagon, driven by a peasant going the same way took gold and, and jewelry and so on. But anyhow, we were walking like that for about three days, through at night-- through nights and so on of very bad bombing and shooting at us from the planes. And just before we got to Warsaw-- maybe 10, 15 miles from Warsaw, from the suburbs of Warsaw-- there was a bombing raid again, and they were machine gunning the people that were walking towards Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I jumped into a ditch next to the road and all of a sudden we hear a racket, a big noise on, on the road. And I say, my God, why the Polish trucks are going with the-- with the army? Why are they making so much noise? We'll be bombed for much longer. And somebody looks ahead and says, they are not Polish trucks. They are German motorcycles. And that's my first encounter with the German army.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as I was sitting there, a, a power that I, I just couldn't believe it existed-- motorized power just passed in front of my eyes. At this time, of course, we didn't know much. It wasn't the same power as now, but seeing all these motorized troops was a tremendous impression on me. And we started going back. We started going back because there was no, no way we could go forward to Warsaw. Warsaw was surrounded at that point. And I came back to my city of Lodz, oh, around the 10th of September, maybe, maybe, maybe 12th, something like that. And as I said, I was sleeping that time at my sister's-- in my sister's apartment. I went back there and my mother was with me. And maybe, I don't know how long after that, they came in. There was posters all over the city that Jews have to wear a yellow armband. The Jews are not allowed to cross or be on the main street, which separated the city into two parts. And I was with my mother in that apartment. I was going around town and fortunately, we had to-- I didn't mind. I was crossing that street anyhow very fast and so on. I was everywhere. And to get money, you know, all I had to do was get into the factory, get a piece of textile goods or something, and take it to the retail store, and I had money. So this is how we provided for ourselves. And then one day there is a knock on the door, and there are some German officers. And we're taking this apartment. You go out. You know, just like this. You just go out. Fortunately, that wasn't our apartment. We could go to our apartment. The winter is approaching, and I knew that because it was a factory in the back, we had coal there. So I will be able to heat during the winter. And I didn't know how long I would be there. Right after they came in, already there were-- there were acts of terrorism and of intimidation of the population. One of them was affected me terribly because my best friend's father, who was one of the Jewish Community Council in Lodz, the whole Community Council was taken out and shot and killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my friend lost his father like this. That was just the beginning right after they came in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Other things were they were grabbing people in the street, grabbing Jews in the street and putting them to work, some dirty work like washing, washing the, stones of the street and so on. I mean, they had fun. They just making fun with the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember, that was even before I had that yellow armband because I was going on the main street with a-- with a Gentile friend of mine who happened to have a very long nose. And a German officer stops us and asks us where is the post office?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we had this-- you know, when you're in your early 20s-- I was 22 at that time-- you like to play jokes. So, of course, we showed him the other way. We wouldn't show him the right way. This was a German. This was the enemy. And he looks at both of us and he says to me, you won't have any trouble, but he turns to the Gentile, you better be careful because you're Jewish. You Jews, you're going to suffer. That was a German officer who stopped us in the streets in September 1939. Very soon thereafter, somebody in the German command obviously wanted to make some money. And there is a poster in the city that says that if you have a Polish foreign passport, they will extend it for a certain amount of money. Now, that was fine except that you had to stand in line in the street and that was in dan-- that was a danger because they were always counting Jews to get to some work or something like that. It was not the danger of life yet at this point. They didn't take anybody to any camps or anything like that, but it was a day work, a full day and a hard work and, you know, with a German guard. And we were not used to it yet, at that point.

speaker: interviewer
text: So but I, I decided to brave it, and I took my passport and my mother's passport. Oh, by the way, when Ribbentrop and Molotov signed the non-aggression pact, my, my family here in the States rushed and got a visa for my mother. They couldn't get a visa for me, but they got a visa for my mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was supposed to go to America on the 3rd of September, but the boat that was supposed to take her didn't come in. So she was there. She was with me in Lodz with the visa to go to the United States. So she had the visa and her passport I had no visa.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I braved this thing and went well and I got my extension of my Polish foreign passport, going to abroad, a special passport. And I also got a visa through Germany, which I didn't know I needed at all. And this was that. So then I was told that to get out of there, I needed a visa of-- I had the passage through Germany. I needed a visa to any country to get out. I started thinking where I can go. A lot of my friends were going east, away from the Germans and trying to penetrate into Russia-- into Soviet Union at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: My feeling was, I want to get to France where we already heard around October maybe, November, that the Polish army is being reconstructed in France and that there will be a Polish divisions of the French army fighting at the front. And I said, that's my-- that's my way. I'm going there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had that and a friend of mine also got his passport extended and his visa to Germany. Which is what is very significant here to the story, is that the visa through Germany expired many days before the extension of the passport expired. They both had expiration dates. And my mother had this, and my mother started that she has to go to America and she has to go to America for a very simple reason. Not to save her life. Her daughter was pregnant, and what mother would stay in Poland under the Germans when her daughter is in very good comfort in America, but is pregnant? So, of course, she has to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as I mentioned, that my mother was an amputee and had an artificial leg. And one day I drove her to the train, and I found out that Jews could not get into passenger cars, that they had to go into freight cars. And I said to my mother, look, other people have no papers. You have papers but you have-- this is not for you. You cannot go this way. She has to go. Anyhow, the next day I took two, two workers from my factory. They placed my mother in a-- in a freight car and went with her all the way to the former Polish-German border. At that point, they told me later on there were already other trains, and they could place her in, in a passenger car. Before she left, I gave an a-- an address of-- a friend of mine married a Jew from Vienna and his parents' address, I had. And I said to my mother, if you have any problems while you are in Vienna-- because she was going to Vienna and then into Italy-- see these people. OK. So my mother is gone. I am all alone. I have to get a visa to any country to get out. So the Italians will let me in. Italy does not want us. I find a Greek consul, who was not a consul anymore, but he has all those stamps. And for an amount of money he gives me an entry visa to Greece with a solemn promise that I will never show my face in Greece. OK. Fine. So here I am this way and my friend, who had the same papers-- both of us, we had the same papers. And in December 1939, before the ghetto was established in Lodz, I get on a train with this friend of mine and we sit in a passenger car, by the way. By that time already, passenger cars were available. And we go. We roll our overcoats. It's cold, so we have overcoats. We roll our overcoats so that my yellow armband doesn't show. And we sit there, and we travel. And soon the compartment-- you know the European compartments are for just few people-- is empty and we just sit, the two of us. And all of a sudden, we are in Germany. The names start changing. And it's absolutely dark. Everything is dark because they were expecting air raids and so on, at the beginning of the war. It never happened. And two gendarmes, German gendarmes, walk into the compartment, and they ask for our papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now what flashed through my head was that my visa through Germany's ex-- expired, so I might have trouble. So I open my passport on the extension of the passport, and I'm holding my thumb on it and I hand it, the two passports. And he looks at me and he says, but you need a visa through Germany. And I said, no I don't. So he says, why don't you? Oh, I say, my city has been incorporated into the German Reich and therefore, I don't need a visa to go through Germany. He scratches his head, scratches his head. And finally, he says all right, and stamps both passports an entry.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, that was miracle number one. You know, we are there and my friend is so happy about it that he has a tablet of chocolate and he hands it to the gendarme, the tablet of chocolate. And I tell the gendarme, listen, do you know what train connection we have to Breslau, Pol-- that was in Germany. Now it's-- the city's named Wroclaw and it's in Poland. And they don't answer. They walk out of the compartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Several stations later, we hear the heavy boots of the gendarmes on the-- on the platform of the station and it's dark. And all of a sudden, there is a flashlight pointing at our compartment. The door opened. They are here, you know, like this. And my life ended at that point, you know? I was absolutely sure something happened to them in their heads.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they sit down, and it's dark, absolutely dark. And this flashlight goes onto his knees and I see a train schedule there. They came to tell us what connection we have from Breslau to Vienna. So miracle number two.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this point, we are very happy. We are amenable to anything. And this man says to me, do you have money? I said, yes, we have money. Do you have food stamps? No, we don't have food stamps. All right, you have money. We have food stamps. We go-- we go to dinner together because you have time in Breslau. And I can tell you one thing. Walking into that restaurant with two German gendarmes who go in and they say Heil Hitler. You know, Heil Hitler. All the time they greet everybody, you know? And we go in, the two poor Jews, already took off the band, and we know that is a crime in Germany to say Heil Hitler if you are Jewish and to raise your hand. So we go and we do kind of gymnastics that were absolutely impossible, you know? That dinner was a nightmare. But we got out, and we are put on a train. We get on a train to Vienna. OK. So we are already a little further out. We get to Vienna, and as we get to Vienna, the train to Tarvisio, which is the border between Italy and Austria-- Germany at that time-- has, has left. When is the next train? Something like 24 hours. So I say to my friend, you know what? Let's go and visit these people where I sent my mother to. And we take a cab, and we go there. And I knock on the door. And the lady opens up, and I introduce myself. And she says, oh, my God, your mother just left yesterday. So I find out-- they invite me in. And this man is a lawyer, a Viennese lawyer. And he starts looking at our papers and says the same thing as with your mother. The Italians are not going to let you in unless you go in either a day before the departure for Greece and you have to prove to them that you're getting out of Italy. How do we prove that? Oh, you need-- you need tickets for a boat from Trieste to Piraeus, which is, you know, the port of Athens. And how do we get that? Do you have money? Yes, we have money. We have Polish zlotys and German marks. That's no good. You need dollars. Where do we get dollars? He says, I tell you where do you get dollars. Your mother, she went and she's in Genoa in a certain hotel. Because my mother also was taken off the train. The Italians returned her to Germany and she was off the train. She was one night in a concentration camp at the border, and then they let her come back to Vienna. And this man helped her with the visas.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, your mother is in this and this hotel in Genoa, and she's waiting for the boat to America. And I think she has about $60 over. And $60 is what we needed exactly. So we will wire your mother, but we cannot wire. We have no right to be there. We have no right to be in Germany. Our visa through Germany is expired.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he says, I'm going to put you in a hotel where they won't ask any questions and the steamship company, the Greek steamship company, has an office in Vienna, and they will wire there.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. So he goes to the steamship company to wire my mother to pay the money. And three days later I go there, to that office, and they give me a little piece of paper, just a receipt that they received $60 towards the passage. OK. I have this. I'm very proud. We say good bye to Mr. and Mrs. Lowe, and we are on a train out of Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: We come to the German border and just before we come, it's full of Italian workers coming home for Christmas. It's just before Christmas. And oh yes, this lawyer told us, don't tell the Italians you're Jewish. They don't know Jews from nothing, you know? They don't know Jews. They are not like Germans or Poles. Poles know. So OK. The German people ask us for papers before we go out, which we didn't expect. And he turns to us and says, are you Jewish? And my good friend, whose name was Goldman, says no, we are not. Rous, off the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: They take us off the train. And I tried to explain to him, look, we-- which was not true, but I'm telling him-- look, we are converted. So to us, we are not Jewish. That's why he said what he said, but, of course, we are Jewish to you because we were-- nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: We go into a little office, Gestapo office at the station, and they start interviewing us, asking us questions. Why are you going to Greece? What are you going to do? So he says he has an uncle in banking business. We're going to work there, and I have to complete my studies, I tell them, at the university. And there is no work in the banking. We were in banking in Lodz and there is no business going on. And they are not convinced. All of a sudden, exactly like in a movie, a guy is sitting in a corner in trench coat and a hat, the whole thing. And he says, do you have tickets to go from Trieste to Piraeus?

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, of course, and I give him this piece of paper and he starts laughing. He says, oh, the Italians won't let them in. Those guys will be back here tomorrow morning. And, you know, you can let them go. And they take us to the train. They put us on a train, and we're rolling. We're rolling and I said to my friend, listen, once we are in Italy, I jump the train. If anything happens, I'm not going back to Germany. We stop at Tarvisio, which is the station, the border station. They take everybody off the train. They are going to go through customs. We go through customs and the train leaves. And the announcement is anybody whose papers that were taken should take the next train and an employee is going to hand you back your passports.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we get on the next train. Absolutely no problem. The guy comes in, Pollacko, Pollacko, hands us our passports and here we are in Trieste. We are in Trieste with Polish zlotys and German marks, which doesn't mean anything, you know. We have no money, basically. So we go to the best hotel. You have no money, you go to the best, right? So we go to the best hotel, and we take a room and the next morning, my, my friend has a golden cigarette case, and we're going to sell that to wire my father for money. And that's what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, I went to the French consulate and I said, I want to get into the army. And a week later my father sent us money. My father sent a letter saying stay in Trieste. I'm working on papers to bring you to the United States, but I was going into the army. I was going to fight.

speaker: interviewer
text: You really wanted to stay and fight the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to fight the Germans, right. And so then, of course, already the French paid for our passage, and we went directly to a camp, Polish camp in Brittany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was called?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was called Coetquidan. It was the Polish first division. And my friend, who was much older than I, and he was-- he had the training in infantry. He went to the infantry, and they assigned me to be an artillery man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we just take a break? In this passport you had from Poland, there was no Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no religion.

speaker: interviewer
text: --identity?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there was no religion. That was-- that was-- all those miracles together. And this was my first escape from German hands. Of course, this is one, one reason why I am still here. Of course, later on I got myself wonder-- wonderful into their hands again. But this was my first escape from German hands. I have been in this camp of Coetquidan for about three months or four months. Three months, I think, till April. No, that's more. It's almost five. From January 1st to April. Let's say four months in training.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there many other Jewish Polish men?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I know of. I didn't meet anybody. In my battery, I was the only one. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, the training was-- it was rough because it was the middle of the winter. I advised my, my family in America-- my mother, by the way, got in. You know, once she left, she left and she came to America and she was here already. And I let my family know that I am in France. I was able to let them know. And I went into training and I trained, and you know. And in April, we were sent to the front.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could we just stop here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in Poland, would you have served in the Polish Army if the war hadn't happened? Would it be expected for young men to serve in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely. There was obli-- obligatory service. However, at 21, I had an operation on my inner ear, and I was postponed for a year. So I would have gone into the army just as the war broke out. And when I went to present myself, I went to present myself, they said, we have not enough equipment for the people that we are calling in, that we are mobilizing. We don't even want you rookies to come in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your desire to serve in the army to fight the Germans connected with your sense of being a Jewish activist, that this was your chance to do what you couldn't do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish activist?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's not very often you hear about Jews serving in the armies. So I wondered--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think that I wanted to defeat Hitler. That was my main purpose. I think-- I thought that he had to be defeated. And I couldn't see myself-- and, by the way, you have to-- we don't know what would have happened. I would have come here. I would have been in the American Army. I could have been killed at the Bulge or anywhere else, you know? All we know is that I have survived. But, you know, how it would have been if I chose the other way, you know, I cannot say that I knew, that I knew what will happen. So I wanted-- at that time, my motivation was to defeat Hitler and come back to Poland. I was planning to go back to Poland and, you know, this was our home. We wouldn't have found ourselves in, in this wonderful country. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were trained to do what job in the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I was-- I was in charge of one 75-millimeter gun and the crew of the gun.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is front line job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Front line, yes. Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you have trained in something else that would have taken you out of the line of action?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they asked me-- they asked me whether I want to go-- because of my education, they asked me whether I want to go to officer's school. And I refused. I wanted to go to the front. Yeah, yeah. And, you know, somehow or other I feel that I made all the right choices. I didn't know it at the time, but I made all the right choices because I am here. And, you know, my, my helmet was shot, you know, had a big hole in it. My jacket was shot at and had big holes in it. I was under, under artillery barrage many times. I was under artillery barrage when I volunteered to unload a truck of ammunition. And, you know, I volunteered because I was-- I was single. So only my parents would cry. There were some guys who had wives and children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the other soldiers in this army in exile know that you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Poles know their Jews. Poles know their Jews. That's one reason that I had to be a much better soldier than anybody else because I had to show them that Jews can be good soldiers. You know, that's why I have been rewarded with Polish Cross of Valor and French Croix de Guerre, you know, at the battlefield. And I was promoted. Because I had to show that I'm better soldier than anybody else although I am a Jew, although I am the pariah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were intent on proving that you could be super soldier.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, whether it was conscious or not conscious or just below the consciousness. I had to prove it, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.
